input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system is architected through Model‑Based Systems Engineering (MBSE). Functional and logical layers are captured with SysML, using block definition diagrams to specify component interfaces and internal block diagrams to detail connector relationships. Executable behaviour is modeled in MathWorks Simulink, which can be translated into C++ or Java code for runtime execution. Integration between modelling layers is facilitated by SysML’s extension mechanisms and Simulink’s execution flow, ensuring that component interactions, data flow, and control logic are coordinated across the entire twin. Resource management is handled by the Simulink simulation engine and the generated code, while coordination across subsystems is achieved through the explicit SysML artifact links that define the system’s architecture.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The digital twin interfaces with external industrial control systems via MQTT broker for real‑time data exchange. Machine data is encapsulated using the Asset Administration Shell (AAS) standard, facilitating interoperability with enterprise systems. The system connects to a Siemens SINUMERIK 840D-SL machine controller as a data source.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models are built using a multi‑level MBSE approach: requirements, functional, logical (SysML IBDs, state machines, MontiArc Automaton), and product/technical layers. Architecture description languages such as ACME, AADL, and MontiArc capture component‑connector relationships and interconnections. Behavioral models are represented by SysML state machines (v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton. Simulation models are linked through MathWorks Simulink or other SM tools, and executable artefacts can be generated in C++ or Java. The Digital Twin constellation integrates these heterogeneous models via a common data management layer that ingests automated one‑way data streams from the physical asset, aggregates contextual traces, and maintains model relationships across the abstraction hierarchy.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Sensor time-series data (floating‑point values) are streamed from the physical system to the digital twin via MQTT. The data are captured as contextual data traces and aggregated according to the digital shadow reference model. Pre‑processing involves converting raw sensor measurements into floating‑point traces, which are stored in the digital shadow.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Communication between the physical system and the digital twin uses MQTT as the transport protocol. No specific MQTT version, QoS level, or additional network topology details are provided.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin development lifecycle follows a sequential abstraction hierarchy—requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layer—each represented with model‑based systems engineering (MBSE) notations. SysML is typically used at the requirements and functional levels, while architectural description languages such as ACME, AADL, or MontiArc describe component‑connector structures at the logical and product layers. Transition between stages is facilitated by reuse of model artifacts, simulation in MathWorks Simulink or MontiArc Automaton to validate behavior prior to hardware integration, and active components that convert one‑way digital shadow data streams into bidirectional interfaces, thereby enabling governance of design change and stakeholder integration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: Engineering and DT evolution are governed by MBSE methodologies including RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics), OOSEM, SPES, and SMArDT. Models are authored in SysML, represented in ADLs (ACME, AADL, MontiArc), and extended with state machines (SysML v1.7) or MontiArc Automaton to capture dynamic behavior. Simulation and validation leverage MathWorks Simulink, while code generation targets C++ or Java environments. Version control is implied by model reuse across abstraction layers and the inclusion of active components that support bidirectional communication, allowing iterative evolution of the twin through successive refinement cycles.

II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', however the document describes specific services provided by the digital shadow, such as 'visualization in a dashboard', 'real-time identification' of joint friction, and the capability to 'improve the robot control’s accuracy'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', yet the text enumerates several tools and technologies used, including 'Asset Administration Shells (AAS)', 'MathWorks MATLAB Simulink' for simulation, 'SysML' for modeling, 'MQTT' for data streaming, and 'UML' for diagrams.

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the methodology and data structure described in the source. It correctly identifies the use of MQTT for streaming time-series data and the storage of floating-point values within data traces. This is supported by the text: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT' and 'every data trace stores an array of torque τ and angular velocity ω as floating point values'. It also correctly references the 'set of contextual data traces' definition from the Digital Shadow Reference Model.

The paper explicitly defines the system implemented—a Digital Shadow—as a passive entity that does not send commands back to the physical system. Quote: 'A digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.' Therefore, the description of control flows from digital to physical is indeed not present in the document for the specific system under study.

The extraction correctly identifies MQTT as the communication protocol used for streaming data. The source states: 'We have access to time series of all sensors, which are streamed via MQTT.' The extraction appropriately notes the absence of further technical details such as specific versions or QoS levels in the text.

The text describes the implementation of the digital shadow, the use of Asset Administration Shells (AAS), and a dashboard, but it does not specify the hosting infrastructure (e.g., cloud, edge, on-premise) or provide details on computational and storage requirements. Quote: 'Our methodology involves modeling the industrial robot’s mechanical structure... and live-data linking as well as visualization in a dashboard.' No specific hosting deployment details are provided.
